issues.description: File::from_raw_fd takes *ownership* of the fd. When the function returns, BufWriter drops, which drops the inner File, which calls close() on the fd. The trailing comment explicitly acknowledges this: 'BufWriter drops here, which drops the inner File, which closes the fd. But the caller expects the fd to remain open for further operations.' This is a latent bug: any caller that subsequently write()s, sendfile()s, or close()s the fd will either operate on a closed descriptor (EBADF) or — far worse — on an unrelated descriptor that the kernel reallocated to the same number after the implicit close. This is a classic fd-reuse race that can silently corrupt data on another open file/socket. The function's contract ('caller is responsible for closing the fd') is therefore violated by the implementation itself.
issues.suggested_fix: Do not take ownership of the fd. Either (a) accept a BorrowedFd<'_> / &impl AsFd and construct a scoped writer that does not close on drop, (b) wrap the File in std::mem::ManuallyDrop so the destructor is suppressed, or (c) call writer.into_inner()?.into_raw_fd() at the end to release ownership back to the caller. Option (a) is the modern idiom: `pub fn export_stl_to_fd(mesh: &Mesh, fd: BorrowedFd<'_>) -> Result<usize>` and write via a wrapper that implements io::Write without owning the fd.

issues.description: `std::os::unix::io::FromRawFd` is a Unix-only import. Reify ships a Tauri GUI and is expected to build on Windows (multiple M6/M7 task memories reference Windows/Tauri targets). This import will fail `cargo check` on any non-Unix target, breaking the entire reify-runtime crate for Windows users. There is no `#[cfg(unix)]` gate on either the import or the function.
issues.suggested_fix: Gate the import, function, and any tests with `#[cfg(unix)]`, and provide a Windows counterpart using `std::os::windows::io::{FromRawHandle, RawHandle}` if cross-platform parity is required. At minimum: `#[cfg(unix)] use std::os::unix::io::{FromRawFd, BorrowedFd};` and `#[cfg(unix)] pub fn export_stl_to_fd(...)`.

issues.description: A new public function that performs unsafe fd manipulation and writes a binary format is added with zero tests. There is no coverage for: (1) round-trip correctness (write-to-pipe, read-back, assert header + triangle count + vertex data match the input mesh), (2) empty-mesh edge case (triangles.len() == 0), (3) non-multiple-of-3 triangle slice (currently silently truncated by chunks(3)), (4) out-of-bounds vertex index (currently panics), (5) fd lifetime — verifying the fd is still usable after the call (which, per the bug above, it is not). Given the unsafe block and the fd ownership bug, tests are the only way to catch this before it reaches users.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a unit test that creates a pipe via `nix::unistd::pipe` (or `libc::pipe`), passes the write end to export_stl_to_fd, reads the full STL from the read end, and asserts: (a) the 80-byte header, (b) tri_count == expected, (c) each vertex matches mesh.vertices(), (d) the write-end fd is still open and writable after the call returns (proves no double-close).

issues.description: The binary-STL serialization (80-byte header, u32 tri count, 12 bytes normal, 9*f32 vertices, u16 attr) is duplicated between export_stl and export_stl_to_fd. Any future bug fix (e.g., computing real normals, validating indices, handling degenerate triangles) will need to be made in two places and will drift.
issues.suggested_fix: Extract a private helper `fn write_stl_binary<W: Write>(mesh: &Mesh, w: &mut W) -> Result<usize>` and call it from both export_stl (with BufWriter<File>) and export_stl_to_fd (with a non-owning fd writer). This also makes the function trivially unit-testable against an in-memory Vec<u8> without any fd plumbing.

issues.description: The SAFETY comment — 'caller guarantees fd is valid and open' — is incomplete. From_raw_fd additionally requires that the caller transfers *exclusive ownership* of the fd (no other File/Socket owns it) and that nothing else will close it concurrently. Since the function's stated contract is the opposite (caller keeps ownership), the SAFETY comment actively misrepresents the invariants and this unsafe block is in fact unsound as written.
issues.suggested_fix: Either switch to a BorrowedFd-based API that needs no unsafe at all, or expand the SAFETY comment to: 'caller transfers exclusive ownership of fd to this function; fd must be open, valid, and not aliased; on return, fd is closed'. Also update the doc comment to match — the current doc says the caller closes it.

issues.description: Indexing with `vertices[idx as usize]` panics on a malformed mesh where a triangle references a vertex index >= vertices.len(). Since this function is exposed over what appears to be an IPC/streaming boundary (the 'streaming to a client' doc), a panic here will take down the runtime thread rather than return an error to the client. The existing codebase convention per memory (feedback_silent_defaults_pattern) is 'noisy errors, not silent defaults' — but a panic at an IPC boundary is the wrong kind of noise.
issues.suggested_fix: Use `.get(idx as usize).ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("triangle index {} out of bounds (len={})", idx, vertices.len()))?` and propagate with `?`. Additionally, validate `triangles.len() % 3 == 0` up front — `chunks(3)` currently silently drops a trailing 1-or-2-element remainder.

issues.description: The hardcoded `50` is the size of one binary-STL facet record (12 normal + 36 vertices + 2 attr). It is not derived from anything written, so if the format code above is ever changed (e.g., to emit computed normals using f64, or to include the attribute count), this running total will silently desync from reality. bytes_written is the function's return value, so a drift here becomes a wrong count reported to the caller — which matters because the caller is presumably using it to frame a length-prefixed message.
issues.suggested_fix: Either compute from a `const FACET_SIZE: usize = 50;`, or, better, wrap the underlying writer in a byte-counting adapter (or use `std::io::Write::write` return values) so bytes_written reflects what was actually written. The simplest fix: `bytes_written = 84 + triangles.len() / 3 * 50;` computed once after the loop.

issues.description: Writing (0,0,0) for the facet normal is technically valid per the binary STL spec (which says viewers should recompute when normal is zero), but many real-world viewers — including several CAD and 3D-printing slicers — treat a zero normal as authoritative and render backwards-facing or invisible facets. The original export_stl function likely computes real normals; degrading them here changes user-visible behavior depending on which export path is taken.
issues.suggested_fix: Compute the face normal as `(v1 - v0).cross(v2 - v0).normalize()` and write it as three f32s. This is ~6 additional lines and removes a footgun. If intentional (for speed on the streaming path), add a doc comment explaining the tradeoff and the known-compatible viewer set.

issues.description: The existing export_stl function (per the diff context) uses `Context` (imported but unused in the new function) to attach error context like 'failed to write STL header'. The new function propagates raw `io::Error`s with `?` and no context, so a failure in the middle of a large mesh produces an opaque 'Broken pipe' with no indication it came from STL export vs. some other IO.
issues.suggested_fix: Add `.context("writing STL header")`, `.context("writing triangle count")`, and `.with_context(|| format!("writing triangle {}", tri_idx))?` on the hot loop. Matches the convention used by export_stl.

issues.description: The function signature `fn(&Mesh, fd: i32) -> Result<usize>` leaks a POSIX-specific primitive into a module named generically `mesh_export`. This couples mesh export to the IPC/transport layer and makes the function unusable over any non-fd destination (Tauri IPC channel, HTTP response, Vec<u8>). The natural split is: mesh_export owns `write_stl_binary<W: Write>`, and the transport layer (whichever crate is doing the streaming) does the fd dance.
issues.suggested_fix: Move the fd-specific adapter into the streaming/IPC crate that needs it, and keep mesh_export purely generic over `Write`. This also eliminates the unsafe block from mesh_export entirely and makes the Windows port free.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The diff introduces a blocking use-after-free: File::from_raw_fd takes ownership of the fd, so when BufWriter drops the fd is closed — yet the function's own trailing comment states the caller expects it to remain open. Any subsequent use by the caller will hit EBADF or, worse, silently operate on a kernel-recycled fd pointing at an unrelated open file/socket. Additionally, the new code is Unix-only but has no `#[cfg(unix)]` gate, which will break Windows builds for the entire reify-runtime crate (Reify ships a Tauri GUI). The function is also entirely untested despite containing unsafe code and a binary serializer. Beyond the blockers, the STL serialization is duplicated from export_stl (extract a `write_stl_binary<W: Write>` helper), the SAFETY comment misrepresents the unsafe invariants, vertex indexing can panic on malformed meshes at an IPC boundary, bytes_written is a hardcoded magic number that can drift from reality, zero normals are hostile to several real-world viewers, error context is missing, and exposing a raw POSIX fd in a generic mesh_export module leaks transport concerns into the wrong layer. Recommend switching to a BorrowedFd-based (or ideally `Write`-generic) API, gating Unix-only code, extracting the shared serializer, and adding a pipe-based round-trip test that proves the fd is still open on return.
